dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 responding for chest pain.
Hi ma'am, I'm Marla, with rescue squad. What brought you to call 911 today?
It's chest pain that shoots to my back.
Can you tell me your name and your birthday?
I see your eyes are open and focused. That's great.
Can you tell me what hospital you've been to recently?
Can you please move your fingers one at a time for me?
I'm going to do a quick check of your airway.
Let me check your breathing.
Your breathing rate is 20 times per minute.
Her blood pressure is 232 over 91-this is pretty high.
Her pulse is 103 beats per minute.
Her oxygen level is at 98%, which is okay for now.
Her radial pulse is strong and regular. Her skin is warm, pale, and dry.
Would you mind telling me a bit about yourself, like medications you're on or past medical conditions?
I've had high blood pressure, heart surgery, I have COPD and anxiety.
Had you been to a doctor recently for something similar?
I saw St. Mary's yesterday for pain in my left breast and was given antibiotics.
On a scale of 0 to 10, how would you describe your chest pain?
It feels like a 10.
Does the pain tend to move?
It goes from my chest into my back.
You're alert and oriented to person, place, and time. Your Glasgow Coma Scale score is 15, which means you're fully alert.
The EKG shows ST elevation in the inferior leads, suggesting a possible STEMI. There's also a right bundle branch block and T wave abnormalities suggesting anterolateral ischemia.
Our records indicate a glucose result of 0-can you confirm you haven't had anything to eat or drink recently?
I'm going to give you 324 mg of aspirin to help with the heart pain.
We just started with a 4-lead EKG to monitor your heart rhythm.
I see you also have a history of atrial fibrillation. Have you had any changes in your symptoms since yesterday?
We've completed a 12-lead EKG as well-this will help confirm our suspected heart findings.
Based on the EKG and your symptoms, we're following the ACS/AMI (STEMI) protocol and arranging transport to St. Mary's.
Her pulse is now 101, breathing is 22, and her blood pressure is 212 over 80.
Her oxygen level is at 95%, and her EKG currently does not show abnormal rhythms.
We'll be heading to St. Mary's with lights and sirens. A cardiac alert is in place.
Ma'am, we are going to move you gently onto the ambu cot now. Would you mind putting your hands behind your head as we assist you?
Straps are securely fastened, and we're ready to leave the residence. Wheels up, we're heading out.
Wheeling you to the ambulance now and getting you into the unit. You'll be comfortable the whole way.
We have called in a full report to the hospital-your doctor and nurse at St. Mary's are prepared for your arrival.
You refused an IV, so nitroglycerin is withheld at this time.
Glucose remains at 0. We will be monitoring closely en route.
We've noted the administration of 324 mg of aspirin under protocol 2-2.
Take the offload cot as we release the straps and position you onto the hospital bed with your assistance.
You have signed the report form. Your medical details are ready for the hospital staff.
You have a central line for dialysis and fistulas in both arms.
Aspirin has been administered. If you experience any difficulty swallowing or throat pain, let us know.
Did you experience any chest pain that didn't radiate or felt different besides this one?
Yes, I've had some non-cardiac chest pain before, like gas pain, but this feels much worse.
Let me reassess the EKG and confirm if there are any new changes.
You mentioned antibiotics were given for the left breast pain. Can you say it was prescribed recently or already taken today?
It was prescribed yesterday but I haven't taken any of it yet.
Great, we can alert the hospital staff to be aware of that.
We'll keep monitoring your vitals en route. You may feel a bit anxious but we're right here with you.
The aspirin has been successfully given. If you experience any difficulty swallowing or pain in your throat, let us know.
Since your chemistry was at 0, we'll monitor more closely in case any changes happen en route.
We are now preparing for the travel. Lights and sirens are on. You'll get to the hospital quickly.
